lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Aristotle, my dear friend. Let us speak of truth itself. Tell me, what is the simplest truth you can imagine?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: That a horse is a horse. A thing is itself.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Excellent. Statements like that, which can be either true or false, are called [proposition|propositions].

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: But surely philosophers don't write every proposition out in full?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed not. We usually give them simple names like $A$, $B$, and $C$. Today, $A$ might mean 'the clouds have gathered.' Tomorrow it could mean something entirely different.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So the letters are merely placeholders for ordinary statements.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. But logic is more than naming propositions. Logic is about **reasoning** — starting from things we accept, and arriving at things we did not yet know.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So we need a way to keep track of what we have assumed and what we have proved.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. Suppose we are trying to prove something. We may temporarily borrow certain statements — treat them as true for the sake of argument — and see what follows.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Even if we do not know whether they are actually true?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Yes. Such a temporary belief is called an **[assume|assumption]**. Inside our reasoning, we may use it freely. But we must remember that it was borrowed — it does not belong to us.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: A sandbox for reasoning! Inside the sandbox, the assumption is real. Outside, it vanishes.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Just so. And a proof is a disciplined record of what follows from what. At every step, we know exactly which assumptions are active and what conclusion they support.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Our proofs have become rather cluttered. We keep saying 'under these assumptions, we have proved this conclusion.'

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Perhaps we should invent a concise notation.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: A kind of shorthand? Mathematicians are rather fond of those.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed. Let us separate the assumptions from the conclusion with a symbol. This little mark, $\vdash$, is called the **[turnstile|judgement turnstile]**.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So everything to the left of the turnstile is what we are temporarily assuming, and everything to the right is what we have successfully proved.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. We call the collection of active assumptions the [context|context], traditionally written as $\Gamma$. Thus, $\Gamma \vdash P$ simply means: 'from the assumptions in $\Gamma$, we have proved $P$.'

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: It is less a statement about the world than a record of the current state of our proof.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: A beautiful observation. Logic is not merely a catalogue of truths; it is a disciplined record of **how** we arrive at them.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So every proof is like a carefully kept journal, never forgetting which assumptions it has borrowed.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. Once an assumption has served its purpose, we may **[discharge|discharge]** it — return it, so to speak — and the conclusion we reached becomes a statement that holds without that assumption.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So assumptions enter, do their work, and then leave. The context grows and shrinks as the proof progresses.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. Now, how shall we speak to the machine?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Machines are rather poor at reading mathematics written for humans. They prefer every expression to have exactly the same shape.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Like building everything out of identical wooden blocks?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Precisely. We represent every expression as a tree made of nested lists. Such a notation is called an **[s-expression|sexpr]**.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: A tree? Then each pair of parentheses is like a branch containing smaller branches.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Exactly. We also place the operation before its arguments. This style is called **[prefix notation|prefix]**.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: So instead of writing $A$ and $B$ with a symbol between them, we write the symbol first, then the arguments, all wrapped in parentheses.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Just so. Every formula and every command follows this same uniform pattern. The machine never has to guess what you mean — the structure is unambiguous.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: And our proof commands follow the same pattern?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Indeed. Every instruction you give is itself an s-expression. The machine parses it, checks it against the rules of logic, and — if it is valid — adds a new line to your proof.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: So I type commands, and the machine replies with judgements?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Exactly. This is called a [repl|REPL] — Read, Evaluate, Print, Loop. You type a command, the machine evaluates it, prints the result, and waits for your next command. Step by step, you build a proof.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: And the machine guarantees that every step is logically valid?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: With perfect mathematical certainty. If the machine accepts your proof, there is no room for error. Each step is checked against the [inference-rules|inference rules] of the system.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Then let us begin. I am ready to write my first command.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Start with the simplest thing imaginable: borrow a truth and observe that you have it. The command is `(assume A)` — it places $A$ in your context, and the machine responds with $\{A\} \vdash A$. You assumed $A$, and therefore you have proved $A$.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: I borrow a lantern, and the machine confirms: in this room, there is light. Then let us step forward and reason.
